data.reasoningText:
Now I understand the project. Let me plan the migration from xUnit.net v2 to v3:

1. **Project file changes:**
   - Replace the three separate packages (`xunit.abstractions`, `xunit.extensibility.core`, `xunit.extensibility.execution`) with `xunit.v3` (the single v3 package for extensibility)
   - Remove `xunit.runner.visualstudio` (v3 uses `xunit.runner.visualstudio` v3 or the built-in runner)
   - Actually, in xUnit v3, test projects are executables, not class libraries. The SDK changes to `Microsoft.NET.Sdk` but the `OutputType` should be `Exe`.
   - Also need to remove `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` since v3 doesn't require it for standalone test executables

2. **Code changes:**
   - The `Xunit.Abstractions` namespace is gone in v3. `ITestOutputHelper` moves directly to the `Xunit` namespace, though it's still available with the same interface.

Let me verify the exact package names and API surface for xUnit.net v3 to make sure I have the migration path correct.

For xUnit.net v3, the package structure is different:
   - `xunit.v3` is the main package for test projects, bundling core, execution, and the runner together
   - `xunit.v3.extensibility` handles extensibility needs
   - `xunit.runner.visualstudio` gets replaced with `xunit.v3.runner.visualstudio`, though v3 projects might not need it since they're executable-based
   - The `xunit.abstractions` package is no longer needed since those types are merged into the core

Since this project uses extensibility APIs like `ITestOutputHelper` and references the extensibility packages, I need to figure out whether `xunit.v3.extensibility` or just `xunit.v3` is the right fit here.

Looking at the v3 migration path: the main test package is `xunit.v3`, and for extensibility it's `xunit.v3.extensibility`. Since this is a test project with test infrastructure, I'll go with `xunit.v3`. The namespace consolidation means `ITestOutputHelper` moves from `Xunit.Abstractions` to just `Xunit`, and I can drop the separate extensibility package references. For v3, test projects become executables, so I'll need to set the output type accordingly and remove the test SDK and runner packages entirely. I'll outline the key migration steps: swap in the `xunit.v3` package, remove the test SDK and runner dependencies, update the using statements to reference just `Xunit`, convert the project to an executable, and adjust the global.json configuration. Then I'll build and verify everything works.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://www.nuget.org/packages/xunit.v3:
*   [README](#readme-body-tab)
*   [Frameworks](#supportedframeworks-body-tab)
*   [Dependencies](#dependencies-body-tab)
*   [Used By](#usedby-body-tab)
*   [Versions](#versions-body-tab)
*   [Release Notes](#releasenotes-body-tab)

## xUnit.net v3

xUnit.net is a developer testing framework, built to support Test Driven Development, with a design goal of extreme simplicity and alignment with framework features. Supports .NET Framework 4.7.2 or later and .NET 8 or later.

Installing this package installs xunit.v3.mtp-v1.

## About xUnit.net

xUnit.net is a free, open source, community-focused unit testing tool for C#, F#, and Visual Basic. xUnit.net v3 supports .NET 8.0 or later, and .NET Framework 4.7.2 or later.

xUnit.net works with the [.NET SDK](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download) command line tools, [Visual Studio](https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/), [Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/), [JetBrains Rider](https://www.jetbrains.com/rider/), [NCrunch](https://www.ncrunch.net/), and any development environment compatible with [Microsoft Testing Platform](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/testing/microsoft-testing-platform-intro) or [VSTest](https://github.com/microsoft/vstest).

xUnit.net is part of the [.NET Foundation](https://www.dotnetfoundation.org/) and operates under their [code of conduct](https://www.dotnetfoundation.org/code-of-conduct). It is licensed under [Apache 2](https://opensource.org/licenses/Apache-2.0) (an OSI approved license). The project is [governed](https://xunit.net/governance) by a Project Lead.

For project documentation, please visit the [xUnit.net project home](https://xunit.net/).

*   _New to xUnit.net? Get started with the [.NET SDK](https://xunit.net/docs/getting-started/v3/getting-started)._
*   _Need some help building the source? See [BUILDING.md](https://github.com/xunit/xunit/tree/main/BUILDING.md)._
*   _Want to contribute to the project? See [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/xunit/.github/tree/main/CONTRIBUTING.md)._
*   _Want to contribute to the assertion library? See the [suggested contribution workflow](https://github.com/xunit/assert.xunit/tree/main/README.md#suggested-contribution-workflow) in the assertion library project, as it is slightly more complex due to code being spread across two GitHub repositories._

[![Powered by NDepend](https://raw.github.com/xunit/media/main/powered-by-ndepend-transparent.png)](http://www.ndepend.com/)

### Latest Builds

Latest stable

Latest CI ([how to use](https://xunit.net/docs/using-ci-builds))

data.reasoningText:
Interesting - the xUnit.net v3 package `xunit.v3` has many versions. The latest stable is `3.2.2`. Let me also check the `xunit.v3.extensibility` package to see what's available, since this project uses extensibility APIs.

Actually, wait. Looking at the version numbers more carefully: the `xunit.v3` package versions don't correspond to xUnit v3. The package name IS `xunit.v3` and the versions are the package versions. So `3.2.2` is the latest stable version of the `xunit.v3` package.

Let me also check `xunit.v3.extensibility` versions.


For a test project using `ITestOutputHelper`, the `xunit.v3` package alone should work. I'll upgrade to version `3.2.2` and remove the older xUnit packages along with the test runners, since v3 uses Microsoft Testing Platform and runs as an executable. I'll also need to clean up the using statements that reference the old abstractions.

Let me check the xUnit v3 getting started guide to confirm the setup.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://xunit.net/docs/getting-started/v3/getting-started:
## Getting Started with xUnit.net v3 2025 August 13

In this document, we will demonstrate getting started with xUnit.net v3 when targeting .NET 8 (or later) and/or .NET Framework 4.7.2 (or later), showing you how to write and run your first set of unit tests. We will be using the .NET SDK command line.

##### Note

The examples were done with C#, xUnit.net v3 2.0.3, .NET SDK 9.0.301, and .NET 8. The version numbers, paths, and generated templates may differ for you, depending on the versions you're using. The instructions for .NET vs. .NET Framework are identical other than picking the appropriate target framework; however, being able to build and run .NET Framework tests on Linux or macOS requires installing [Mono](https://www.mono-project.com/download/stable/) first. See the [Multi-targeting with non-Windows OSes](/docs/getting-started/multi-target/non-windows) documentation for more information.

## Download the .NET SDK

The .NET SDK is available for [download](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download) for Windows, Linux, and macOS.

Once you've downloaded and installed the SDK, open a fresh command prompt of your choice (CMD, PowerShell, bash, etc.) and make sure that you can access the command line by typing `dotnet --version`. You should be rewarded with a single line, describing the version of the .NET SDK you have installed:

```
$ dotnet --version
9.0.301
```

##### Note

The first time you run the `dotnet` command, it may perform some post-installation steps. Once these one-time actions are done, it will execute your command.

## Install the .NET SDK templates

We ship templates for creating new projects, and they must be installed before they can be used.

From your command prompt, type: `dotnet new install xunit.v3.templates`

You should see the output similar to this:

```
$ dotnet new install xunit.v3.templates
The following template packages will be installed:
   xunit.v3.templates

Success: xunit.v3.templates::2.0.3 installed the following templates:
Template Name                   Short Name        Language    Tags
------------------------------  ----------------  ----------  ----------
xUnit.net v3 Extension Project  xunit3-extension  [C#],F#,VB  Test/xUnit
xUnit.net v3 Test Project       xunit3            [C#],F#,VB  Test/xUnit
```

As of this writing, we ship two templates (`xunit3` and `xunit3-extension`), in three languages (C#, F#, and VB.NET).

## Create the unit test project

From the command line, create a folder for your test project, change into it, and then create the project using `dotnet new`:

```
$ mkdir MyFirstUnitTests
$ cd MyFirstUnitTests
$ dotnet new xunit3
The template "xUnit.net v3 Test Project" was created successfully.

Processing post-creation actions...
Restoring .../MyFirstUnitTests/MyFirstUnitTests.csproj:
Restore succeeded.
```

The generated project file should look something like this:

```
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <RootNamespace>MyFirstUnitTests</RootNamespace>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <!--
    To enable the Microsoft Testing Platform 'dotnet test' experience, add property:
      <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>

    To enable the Microsoft Testing Platform native command line experience, add property:
      <UseMicrosoftTestingPlatformRunner>true</UseMicrosoftTestingPlatformRunner>

    For more information on Microsoft Testing Platform support in xUnit.net, please visit:
      https://xunit.net/docs/getting-started/v3/microsoft-testing-platform
    -->
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Content Include="xunit.runner.json" CopyToOutputDirectory="PreserveNewest" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Using Include="Xunit" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.13.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.v3" Version="2.0.3" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="3.1.1" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

Let's quickly review what's in this project file:

*   `ImplicitUsings` enables implicit `using` statements in your project. In addition to the default set of implicit `using` statements, you can see below that we've added an implicit `using` for the `Xunit` namespace where the most common xUnit.net types come from. _[More information about implicit usings](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/welcome-to-csharp-10/#global-and-implicit-usings)_
    
*   `Nullable` is enabled in this default template. Our libraries including nullable annotations to help you find when you may be accidentally dealing with `null` values. For example, `Assert.NotNull` is decorated in such a way that the compiler knows, if this assertion did not fail, then the value passed to it is known to not be `null`. _[More information about nullable reference types](https://learn.microsoft.com/do

<note>Content truncated. Call the fetch tool with a start_index of 5000 to get more content.</note>

## Getting Started with xUnit.net v3 2025 August 13

In this document, we will demonstrate getting started with xUnit.net v3 when targeting .NET 8 (or later) and/or .NET Framework 4.7.2 (or later), showing you how to write and run your first set of unit tests. We will be using the .NET SDK command line.

The instructions for .NET vs. .NET Framework are identical other than picking the appropriate target framework; however, being able to build and run .NET Framework tests on Linux or macOS requires installing [Mono](https://www.mono-project.com/download/stable/) first. See the [Multi-targeting with non-Windows OSes](/docs/getting-started/multi-target/non-windows) documentation for more information.

## Download the .NET SDK

The .NET SDK is available for [download](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download) for Windows, Linux, and macOS.

Once you've downloaded and installed the SDK, open a fresh command prompt of your choice (CMD, PowerShell, bash, etc.) and make sure that you can access the command line by typing `dotnet --version`. You should be rewarded with a single line, describing the version of the .NET SDK you have installed:

```
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <RootNamespace>MyFirstUnitTests</RootNamespace>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <!--
    To enable the Microsoft Testing Platform 'dotnet test' experience, add property:
      <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>

    To enable the Microsoft Testing Platform native command line experience, add property:
      <UseMicrosoftTestingPlatformRunner>true</UseMicrosoftTestingPlatformRunner>

    For more information on Microsoft Testing Platform support in xUnit.net, please visit:
      https://xunit.net/docs/getting-started/v3/microsoft-testing-platform
    -->
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Content Include="xunit.runner.json" CopyToOutputDirectory="PreserveNewest" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Using Include="Xunit" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.13.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.v3" Version="2.0.3" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="3.1.1" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

Let's quickly review what's in this project file:

*   `ImplicitUsings` enables implicit `using` statements in your project. In addition to the default set of implicit `using` statements, you can see below that we've added an implicit `using` for the `Xunit` namespace where the most common xUnit.net types come from. _[More information about implicit usings](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/welcome-to-csharp-10/#global-and-implicit-usings)_
    
*   `Nullable` is enabled in this default template. Our libraries including nullable annotations to help you find when you may be accidentally dealing with `null` values. For example, `Assert.NotNull` is decorated in such a way that the compiler knows, if this assertion did not fail, then the value passed to it is known to not be `null`. _[More information about nullable reference types](https://learn.microsoft.com/do

result.detailedContent:
Contents of https://xunit.net/docs/getting-started/v3/getting-started:
## Getting Started with xUnit.net v3 2025 August 13

In this document, we will demonstrate getting started with xUnit.net v3 when targeting .NET 8 (or later) and/or .NET Framework 4.7.2 (or later), showing you how to write and run your first set of unit tests. We will be using the .NET SDK command line.

##### Note

The examples were done with C#, xUnit.net v3 2.0.3, .NET SDK 9.0.301, and .NET 8. The version numbers, paths, and generated templates may differ for you, depending on the versions you're using. The instructions for .NET vs. .NET Framework are identical other than picking the appropriate target framework; however, being able to build and run .NET Framework tests on Linux or macOS requires installing [Mono](https://www.mono-project.com/download/stable/) first. See the [Multi-targeting with non-Windows OSes](/docs/getting-started/multi-target/non-windows) documentation for more information.

## Download the .NET SDK

The .NET SDK is available for [download](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download) for Windows, Linux, and macOS.

Once you've downloaded and installed the SDK, open a fresh command prompt of your choice (CMD, PowerShell, bash, etc.) and make sure that you can access the command line by typing `dotnet --version`. You should be rewarded with a single line, describing the version of the .NET SDK you have installed:

```
$ dotnet --version
9.0.301
```

##### Note

The first time you run the `dotnet` command, it may perform some post-installation steps. Once these one-time actions are done, it will execute your command.

## Install the .NET SDK templates

We ship templates for creating new projects, and they must be installed before they can be used.

From your command prompt, type: `dotnet new install xunit.v3.templates`

You should see the output similar to this:

```
$ dotnet new install xunit.v3.templates
The following template packages will be installed:
   xunit.v3.templates

Success: xunit.v3.templates::2.0.3 installed the following templates:
Template Name                   Short Name        Language    Tags
------------------------------  ----------------  ----------  ----------
xUnit.net v3 Extension Project  xunit3-extension  [C#],F#,VB  Test/xUnit
xUnit.net v3 Test Project       xunit3            [C#],F#,VB  Test/xUnit
```

As of this writing, we ship two templates (`xunit3` and `xunit3-extension`), in three languages (C#, F#, and VB.NET).

## Create the unit test project

From the command line, create a folder for your test project, change into it, and then create the project using `dotnet new`:

```
$ mkdir MyFirstUnitTests
$ cd MyFirstUnitTests
$ dotnet new xunit3
The template "xUnit.net v3 Test Project" was created successfully.

Processing post-creation actions...
Restoring .../MyFirstUnitTests/MyFirstUnitTests.csproj:
Restore succeeded.
```

The generated project file should look something like this:

```
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <RootNamespace>MyFirstUnitTests</RootNamespace>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <!--
    To enable the Microsoft Testing Platform 'dotnet test' experience, add property:
      <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>

    To enable the Microsoft Testing Platform native command line experience, add property:
      <UseMicrosoftTestingPlatformRunner>true</UseMicrosoftTestingPlatformRunner>

    For more information on Microsoft Testing Platform support in xUnit.net, please visit:
      https://xunit.net/docs/getting-started/v3/microsoft-testing-platform
    -->
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Content Include="xunit.runner.json" CopyToOutputDirectory="PreserveNewest" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Using Include="Xunit" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.13.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.v3" Version="2.0.3" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="3.1.1" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

Let's quickly review what's in this project file:

*   `ImplicitUsings` enables implicit `using` statements in your project. In addition to the default set of implicit `using` statements, you can see below that we've added an implicit `using` for the `Xunit` namespace where the most common xUnit.net types come from. _[More information about implicit usings](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/welcome-to-csharp-10/#global-and-implicit-usings)_
    
*   `Nullable` is enabled in this default template. Our libraries including nullable annotations to help you find when you may be accidentally dealing with `null` values. For example, `Assert.NotNull` is decorated in such a way that the compiler knows, if this assertion did not fail, then the value passed to it is known to not be `null`. _[More information about nullable reference types](https://learn.microsoft.com/do

<note>Content truncated. Call the fetch tool with a start_index of 5000 to get more content.</note>

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a clear picture. Looking at the official xUnit.net v3 template:

1. The project needs `<OutputType>Exe</OutputType>` 
2. The package references are:
   - `xunit.v3` version `2.0.3` (this is the current latest stable matching what they show)
   - `xunit.runner.visualstudio` version `3.1.1` (this is the v3 runner!)
   - `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` version `17.13.0` (still needed for VSTest compat)

Wait, but I checked the NuGet versions and `xunit.v3` has versions like `3.2.2`. Let me re-check... The template says `2.0.3`. That seems like the xUnit.net v3 framework version 2.0.3. 

The getting started page confirms it: xUnit.net v3 2.0.3 is the documented stable version they tested with, so I'll go with that rather than chasing newer versions. I'll stick with the documented version 2.0.3 for `xunit.v3` since that's what the template uses, and update `xunit.runner.visualstudio` to 3.1.1. The main changes needed are adding `<OutputType>Exe</OutputType>` to the project file, swapping out the old packages for the new xunit.v3 ones, and updating the using statements in the code.

data.content:
Now I have the full picture. Let me apply the migration.

**Key changes:**
1. Replace the 3 v2 extensibility packages with `xunit.v3`
2. Upgrade `xunit.runner.visualstudio` to v3
3. Add `<OutputType>Exe</OutputType>` (v3 tests are executables)
4. Remove `using Xunit.Abstractions;` (`ITestOutputHelper` moved to `Xunit` namespace)

arguments.old_str:
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.abstractions" Version="2.0.3" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.extensibility.core" Version="2.9.3" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.extensibility.execution" Version="2.9.3" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.2" />
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="18.3.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
arguments.new_str:
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.v3" Version="3.2.2" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="3.1.5" />
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="18.3.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
So the latest stable `xunit.runner.visualstudio` is `3.1.5`. And the latest stable `xunit.v3` is `3.2.2`.

Now let me plan the migration:

1. **csproj changes:**
   - Add `<OutputType>Exe</OutputType>`
   - Replace 3 old packages with `xunit.v3` version `3.2.2`
   - Update `xunit.runner.visualstudio` to `3.1.5`
   - Keep `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` (template shows it's still needed)


2. **Code changes:**
   - Remove the `using Xunit.Abstractions;` statement since `ITestOutputHelper` moved into the main `Xunit` namespace
3. **global.json:**
   - Leave it unchanged or update if needed

Let me start making these changes.

data.content:
Migration complete. Here's what changed:

**TestProject.csproj:**
- Replaced `xunit.abstractions`, `xunit.extensibility.core`, and `xunit.extensibility.execution` with single `xunit.v3` (3.2.2)
- Upgraded `xunit.runner.visualstudio` from 2.8.2 → 3.1.5
- Added `<OutputType>Exe</OutputType>` (v3 test projects are executables)

**CustomDiscoverer.cs:**
- Removed `using Xunit.Abstractions;` — `ITestOutputHelper` now lives in the `Xunit` namespace
